title: DataFrames: pandas Picks Up Where SQL Left Off
learningObjectives: Load a SQL query result or CSV into a pandas DataFrame
Select columns and rows using label-based (.loc) and position-based (.iloc) indexing
Explain the relationship between a DataFrame, its index, and the Series that make up its columns
Convert between a list of dictionaries and a DataFrame in both directions
whyItMatters: SQL answers a query and hands back rows; pandas is where you actually explore, reshape, and combine that data in Python before it becomes a machine learning feature. Every one of Mission 3's forecasting lessons assumes the data already lives in a DataFrame, not a raw SQL result set.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's analysis layer — the DataFrame that sits between 'query the database' and 'feed a model,' where the actual feature engineering for Mission 3 will happen.

problem.summary: SQL rows alone don't let you reshape or compute new columns easily.
problem.content: Your daily summary SQL query returns rows you can print, but you can't easily do things like 'compute a new column from two existing ones' or 'filter and then plot' without leaving SQL and writing Python by hand row by row — exactly the manual work Mission 1's lists and loops already showed doesn't scale.

motivation.summary: Every Mission 3 forecasting technique expects a DataFrame, not raw rows.
motivation.content: pandas is the tool that sits between a database and a machine learning model — every forecasting technique in Mission 3 expects a DataFrame as input, not raw SQL rows or a Python list of dictionaries.

theory.summary: A DataFrame is a table of Series, selectable by label (.loc) or position (.iloc).
theory.content: A `DataFrame` is a table: rows indexed by an `Index`, columns each stored as a `Series`. `pd.DataFrame(list_of_dicts)` builds one directly from the exact list-of-dictionaries shape used throughout Mission 1's ingestion pipeline. `.loc[row_label, column_label]` selects by label; `.iloc[row_position, column_position]` selects by integer position — mixing the two up is a common source of subtly wrong selections. `df['demand_kw']` returns a single column as a Series; `df[['demand_kw', 'source']]` returns a DataFrame with just those columns.

visualExplanation.summary: A DataFrame is a grid with a labeled index and columns; .loc and .iloc select the same cells two ways.
visualExplanation.content: A DataFrame appears as a grid with a labeled index down the left and labeled columns across the top, with .loc highlighting a label-based selection and .iloc highlighting the equivalent position-based selection on the same cells.

interactiveDemo.summary: Click cells and see the matching .loc and .iloc expressions side by side.
interactiveDemo.content: Click cells in a live DataFrame grid and see the exact `.loc` and `.iloc` expression that would select them, side by side.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: build a DataFrame from records and select with .loc/.iloc.
guidedCoding.content:
```python
import pandas as pd

readings = [
    {"timestamp": "2026-07-01T09:00", "demand_kw": 4821, "source": "substation-12"},
    {"timestamp": "2026-07-01T10:00", "demand_kw": 4790, "source": "substation-12"},
]
df = pd.DataFrame(readings)

print(df.loc[0, "demand_kw"])
print(df.iloc[0, 1])
print(df[["timestamp", "demand_kw"]])
```

independentCoding.summary: Build a DataFrame from battery readings and select a Series and a sub-DataFrame.
independentCoding.content: Given a small list of dictionaries representing battery readings, build a DataFrame, then select the `soc_percent` column alone as a Series and the first two rows as a DataFrame using `.iloc`.

debugging.summary: Fix incorrect row selection caused by mixing .loc and .iloc on a non-default index.
debugging.content: `df.loc[0]` is used to get 'the first row,' but the DataFrame's index isn't plain integers `0,1,2,...`, so it silently selects the wrong row or raises a `KeyError`. Fix by using the correct accessor for the actual index type.

reflection.summary: .loc and .iloc diverge once the index isn't default integers.
reflection.content: Why did `.loc` and `.iloc` give different results once the DataFrame's index was no longer plain 0,1,2,...?

teachBack.summary: Explain the difference between a Series and a one-column DataFrame.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone the difference between `df['demand_kw']` (a Series) and `df[['demand_kw']]` (a DataFrame with one column), and why that distinction actually matters for what methods you can call next.

miniProject.summary: Rebuild the Mission 1 rolling window as a DataFrame.
miniProject.content: Load your Mission 1 sensor-intake mini project's rolling window into a DataFrame instead of a list of dictionaries, and reproduce the same min/max/most-recent report using DataFrame selection instead of manual loops.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This is the shape all of Mission 3's forecasting work will build on.
portfolioEvidence.content: This DataFrame-based rolling window replaces the manual list-of-dictionaries version from Mission 1 — the shape all of Mission 3's forecasting work will build on.

labs.title: loc & iloc
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise requiring correct label-based and position-based selection on a DataFrame with a non-default index, including a deliberate loc/iloc mix-up to diagnose.

simulations.title: DataFrame Selection Visualizer
simulations.description: Clickable DataFrame grid showing the matching .loc and .iloc expression for any selected cell or range.

flashcards.back: .loc selects by label (the index/column names), while .iloc selects by integer position, regardless of what the labels actually are.

flashcards.front: What type of object does df['demand_kw'] return, versus df[['demand_kw']]?
flashcards.back: df['demand_kw'] returns a Series (one-dimensional). df[['demand_kw']] returns a DataFrame containing just that one column (two-dimensional).

rubric: Correctly diagnoses and fixes a .loc/.iloc mix-up caused by a non-default index
Correctly distinguishes Series-returning vs. DataFrame-returning selection syntax
Mini-project reproduces the Mission 1 report's results exactly using DataFrame operations

references.title: pandas User Guide — Intro to data structures: DataFrame
